Helium was discovered in the Sun before it was found on Earth, spotted as a strange yellow line in sunlight during an eclipse in 1868.
A single teaspoon of soil can contain more microorganisms than there are people on Earth.
Bananas are slightly radioactive because they contain potassium-40, a naturally occurring radioactive isotope.
Lightning is roughly five times hotter than the surface of the Sun, reaching about 30,000 degrees Celsius.
Water is one of the few substances that expands when it freezes, which is why ice floats and pipes burst.
Diamond and graphite are both pure carbon. The only difference is how the atoms are arranged.
Sharks predate trees. They have existed for about 400 million years, while the first trees appeared around 350 million years ago.
Octopuses have blue blood because they use copper-based hemocyanin to carry oxygen instead of iron-based hemoglobin.
Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature, and it was once used to make felt hats.
Antarctica is technically the largest desert on Earth, because a desert is defined by how little precipitation it gets.
Some bamboo species can grow nearly a meter in a single day, making them among the fastest-growing plants alive.
Your stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve metal, which is why the stomach lining replaces itself every few days.
Early cyanobacteria flooded the atmosphere with oxygen, causing a mass extinction called the Great Oxidation Event.
The element gold is thought to have formed in the collisions of neutron stars long before the Earth existed.
Glass is neither a simple solid nor a liquid. Physicists classify it as an amorphous solid with a disordered structure.
A hurricane can release more energy in a day than the entire world uses in electricity in a year.
Volcanic lightning happens when ash particles rub together in an eruption plume and build up an electric charge.
Human bone is, ounce for ounce, stronger than steel. A matchbox-sized block can support many tons.
The Earth's inner core is about as hot as the surface of the Sun, roughly 5,400 degrees Celsius.
Cheese is one of the oldest human foods. Traces of cheesemaking date back more than 7,000 years.
Peanuts are not nuts. They are legumes, more closely related to beans and lentils than to almonds or walnuts.
Spider silk is stronger than steel of the same thickness and tougher than Kevlar.
The smell of rain has a name. It is called petrichor, produced by oils and bacteria in the soil.
Nitrogen makes up about 78 percent of the air you breathe, far more than oxygen.
Bees can recognize individual human faces, learning combinations of features much as we do.
One of the largest living organisms on Earth is a fungus in Oregon that spreads across nearly 10 square kilometers.
Hot water can freeze faster than cold water under some conditions, an effect named after a Tanzanian student, Mpemba.
Chalk is made almost entirely of the compressed shells of tiny ancient sea creatures called coccolithophores.
There is enough gold dissolved in the world's oceans to give every person on Earth several kilograms of it.
Corn kernels pop because each one holds a tiny drop of water that flashes to steam and bursts the hull.
The human body contains enough carbon to make around 900 pencils.
Lobsters were once considered poverty food and were fed to prisoners and servants in colonial America.
Some frogs can freeze almost solid in winter, stopping their hearts entirely, then thaw and hop away in spring.
A lightning strike can create tubes of fused sand called fulgurites, sometimes reaching meters underground.
The Dead Sea is so salty that you cannot sink in it. The dense water floats swimmers effortlessly.
Caffeine is a natural pesticide that plants produce to paralyze and kill insects that try to eat them.
Table salt is made of two elements deadly alone: sodium reacts violently with water and chlorine is a poison gas.
Redwood trees can pull water more than 100 meters up from their roots to their highest leaves.
The Earth's magnetic poles wander and have completely flipped hundreds of times over geologic history.
Venus rains sulfuric acid, though it evaporates before reaching the ground due to the intense surface heat.
Some deep-sea bacteria live off chemicals from hydrothermal vents and never need sunlight at all.
Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, staying solid past 3,400 degrees Celsius.
Pumice is a rock that floats on water, because it is full of trapped gas bubbles from volcanic eruptions.
Your body produces about 25 million new cells every second as it constantly renews itself.
Carbon dioxide turns directly from solid to gas without becoming liquid, which is why dry ice smokes.
The world's oldest known living tree is a bristlecone pine in California that is over 4,800 years old.
A single lightning strike heats the surrounding air so fast it explodes outward, and that shockwave is thunder.
Whales evolved from land mammals that walked on four legs and returned to the sea about 50 million years ago.
Iceland is growing wider by about two centimeters a year as the North American and Eurasian plates pull apart.
Grapes throw off sparks in a microwave because their size and water content focus the waves into plasma.
The average cloud weighs around a million pounds, held aloft by rising warm air.
Cyanide smells like almonds, but only about half of people carry the gene that lets them detect it.
Sloths move so slowly that algae grows on their fur, giving them a greenish tint that helps them hide.
Obsidian volcanic glass can be flaked to an edge many times sharper than a surgical steel scalpel.
Neon signs glow because electricity excites neon atoms, and pure neon always shines a distinctive orange-red.
A river in Peru called the Boiling River runs naturally hot enough to cook animals that fall in.
Sunflowers track the Sun across the sky when young, a motion called heliotropism.
Platinum is so rare that all the platinum ever mined would fit inside an average living room.
Krakatoa's 1883 eruption was heard nearly 5,000 kilometers away, one of the loudest sounds in recorded history.
Certain jellyfish can revert to an earlier life stage, effectively cheating death and earning the name immortal jellyfish.
The mineral quartz generates a tiny electric voltage when squeezed, a property used in watches and lighters.
Trees share resources through underground fungal networks, trading nutrients and sending warning signals.
Earth's atmosphere had almost no free oxygen for its first two billion years of existence.
Snowflakes are almost never perfectly symmetrical up close, because each arm grows in slightly different air.
The human eye can distinguish roughly ten million different colors.
Volcanic eruptions can cool the whole planet. The 1815 Tambora eruption caused a year without a summer.
Rust is simply iron combining with oxygen, the same basic reaction as burning, just far slower.
The deepest hole ever drilled, the Kola Superdeep Borehole, reached only about a third of the way through the crust.
Cabbage, broccoli, kale, cauliflower, and Brussels sprouts are all the same plant species, bred into different shapes.
Water can boil and freeze at the same time at a specific pressure and temperature called the triple point.
Some bacteria survive inside solid rock kilometers underground, living on hydrogen for millions of years.
Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backward, beating their wings up to 80 times per second.
Firework colors come from metal salts: copper burns blue-green, strontium red, and sodium yellow.
Big earthquakes can make the entire planet ring like a bell, vibrating for days at frequencies too low to hear.
A newborn baby has around 300 bones, but many fuse together, leaving adults with 206.
Lake Nyos in Cameroon suffocated over 1,700 people in 1986 when it belched a huge cloud of carbon dioxide.
The oldest rocks on Earth are around 4 billion years old, nearly as old as the planet itself.
Wasabi's burn attacks your nose, not your tongue, because its compounds vaporize and hit your sinuses.
There are more atoms in a single glass of water than there are glasses of water in all the oceans.
Penicillin was discovered by accident when mold blew into a petri dish and killed the surrounding bacteria.
Geckos can walk on ceilings thanks to millions of microscopic hairs that grip using molecular forces.
The pressure at the bottom of the ocean's deepest trench is over 1,000 times that at sea level.
Lightning strikes the Earth about 8 million times a day, roughly 100 times every second.
Aluminum was once more valuable than gold, and Napoleon reportedly reserved aluminum cutlery for his finest guests.
Bombardier beetles defend themselves by mixing chemicals to spray a boiling, caustic jet at attackers.
The Sahara desert was green and dotted with lakes as recently as 6,000 years ago.
Chlorophyll makes plants green by absorbing red and blue light and reflecting the green our eyes see.
A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about a billion tons on Earth.
The Venus flytrap can count. It only snaps shut after its trigger hairs are touched twice in quick succession.
Gold is so soft and malleable that a single gram can be hammered into a sheet a square meter in area.
Mount Everest is still rising a few millimeters each year as the Indian plate pushes into Asia.
The human body glows faintly with visible light, far too dim for our eyes to detect.
Distilled water is a poor conductor of electricity. Dissolved minerals in tap water are what carry the current.
Geysers like Old Faithful erupt because groundwater trapped near hot rock flashes to steam and blasts upward.
The oxygen you breathe was once locked inside water molecules split apart by plants during photosynthesis.
Cork is harvested from the bark of a living oak tree that regrows it, so no tree is cut down.
A flash of green sometimes appears at sunset when the atmosphere bends the Sun's last light like a prism.
Blood is red because of iron in hemoglobin, but horseshoe crabs have blue blood used to test vaccines for safety.
The largest crystals ever found, in Mexico's Cave of Crystals, are gypsum beams over 11 meters long.
Wildfires can create their own weather, spawning towering clouds and even fire tornadoes.
Every carbon atom in your body was forged inside a dying star long before the Sun formed.
Marie Curie's notebooks are still so radioactive that they are kept in lead-lined boxes a century later.
